question: B-3. Suppose that we have \( n \) events \( A_{1}, \ldots, A_{n} \), each of which has probability at least \( 1-a \) of occurring, where \( a<1 / 4 \). Further suppose that \( A_{1} \) and \( A \), are mutually independent if \( |i-j|>1 \), although \( A_{1} \) and \( A_{i+1} \) may be dependent. Assume as known that the recurrence \( u_{k+1}=u_{k}-a u_{k-1}, u_{0}=1, u_{1}=1-a \), defines positive real numbers \( u_{k} \) for \( k=0,1, \ldots \). Show that the probability of all of \( A_{1}, \ldots, A_{n} \) occurring is at least \( u_{n} \).
solution:
B-3.
The statement to be proved is false for \( n \geqq 5 \) unless the hypothesis is strengthened to state that \( A_{\text {, }} \) is independent of the conjunction of \( A_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{t-2} \) for \( 3 \leqq i \leqq n \).

The following counterexample with \( n=5 \) was furnished by Professor David M. Bloom of Brooklyn College. Let \( h=33 / 37 \) and \( k=1 /(64+h) \). Let \( P\left(A_{i}\right) \) be the sum of the numbers in the second row of the following table for which \( A_{i} \) appears in the heading:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|}
A_{1} A_{2} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{4} & A_{1} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{1} A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{1} A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} & A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} \\
12 k & 3 k & 6 k & 7 k & 12 k & 6 k
\end{array} \right\rvert\,
\]

Then each \( P\left(A_{i}\right) \) is \( 49 k \) and \( P\left(A_{i} \wedge A_{i}\right)=37 k \) for all \( i, j \) with \( |i-j|>1 \). Since \( (49 k)^{2}=37 k \), the original independence hypothesis holds. Also, \( P\left(A_{i}\right)=1-a \), where \( a=(15+h) k<1 / 4 \). However, for any \( a \leqq 1 / 4 \), we have \( u_{5} \geqq 7 / 64 \) and \( P\left(A_{1} \wedge A_{2} \wedge A_{3} \wedge A_{4} \wedge A_{5}\right)=7 k<7 / 64 \).

descriptive_long.question: B-3. Suppose that we have \( eventcount \) events \( eventone, \ldots, eventlast \), each of which has probability at least \( 1-deviation \) of occurring, where \( deviation<1 / 4 \). Further suppose that \( eventone \) and \( A \), are mutually independent if \( |indexi-indexj|>1 \), although \( eventone \) and \( A_{indexi+1} \) may be dependent. Assume as known that the recurrence \( unextval=ucurrent-deviation uprevious, uinitial=1, ufirst=1-deviation \), defines positive real numbers \( ucurrent \) for \( indexk=0,1, \ldots \). Show that the probability of all of \( eventone, \ldots, eventlast \) occurring is at least \( u_{eventcount} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3.
The statement to be proved is false for \( eventcount \geqq 5 \) unless the hypothesis is strengthened to state that \( A_{\text {, }} \) is independent of the conjunction of \( eventone, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{t-2} \) for \( 3 \leqq indexi \leqq eventcount \).

The following counterexample with \( eventcount=5 \) was furnished by Professor David M. Bloom of Brooklyn College. Let \( ratioh=33 / 37 \) and \( indexk=1 /(64+ratioh) \). Let \( probability\left(eventith\right) \) be the sum of the numbers in the second row of the following table for which \( eventith \) appears in the heading:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|}
 eventone A_{2} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{4} & eventone A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & eventone A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & eventone A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} & A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} \\
 12 indexk & 3 indexk & 6 indexk & 7 indexk & 12 indexk & 6 indexk
\end{array} \right\rvert\,
\]

Then each \( probability\left(eventith\right) \) is \( 49 indexk \) and \( probability\left(eventith \wedge eventith\right)=37 indexk \) for all \( indexi, indexj \) with \( |indexi-indexj|>1 \). Since \( (49 indexk)^{2}=37 indexk \), the original independence hypothesis holds. Also, \( probability\left(eventith\right)=1-deviation \), where \( deviation=(15+ratioh) indexk<1 / 4 \). However, for any \( deviation \leqq 1 / 4 \), we have \( u_{5} \geqq 7 / 64 \) and \( probability\left(eventone \wedge A_{2} \wedge A_{3} \wedge A_{4} \wedge A_{5}\right)=7 indexk<7 / 64 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-3. Suppose that we have \( coppertwig \) events \( sandstone, \ldots, riverbank \), each of which has probability at least \( 1-cloudberry \) of occurring, where \( cloudberry<1 / 4 \). Further suppose that \( sandstone \) and \( A \), are mutually independent if \( |willowstem-cedargrain|>1 \), although \( sandstone \) and \( A_{willowstem+1} \) may be dependent. Assume as known that the recurrence \( butterleaf=dandelion-cloudberry dragonvine, moonflower=1, starluster=1-cloudberry \), defines positive real numbers \( dandelion \) for \( marigold=0,1, \ldots \). Show that the probability of all of \( sandstone, \ldots, riverbank \) occurring is at least \( u_{coppertwig} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3.
The statement to be proved is false for \( coppertwig \geqq 5 \) unless the hypothesis is strengthened to state that \( A_{\text {, }} \) is independent of the conjunction of \( sandstone, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{t-2} \) for \( 3 \leqq willowstem \leqq coppertwig \).

The following counterexample with \( coppertwig=5 \) was furnished by Professor David M. Bloom of Brooklyn College. Let \( silverfin=33 / 37 \) and \( marigold=1 /(64+silverfin) \). Let \( gildedark\left(meadowland\right) \) be the sum of the numbers in the second row of the following table for which \( meadowland \) appears in the heading:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|}
sandstone A_{2} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{4} & sandstone A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & sandstone A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & sandstone A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} & A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} \\
12 marigold & 3 marigold & 6 marigold & 7 marigold & 12 marigold & 6 marigold
\end{array} \right\rvert\,
\]

Then each \( gildedark\left(meadowland\right) \) is \( 49 marigold \) and \( gildedark\left(meadowland \wedge meadowland\right)=37 marigold \) for all \( willowstem, cedargrain \) with \( |willowstem-cedargrain|>1 \). Since \( (49 marigold)^{2}=37 marigold \), the original independence hypothesis holds. Also, \( gildedark\left(meadowland\right)=1-cloudberry \), where \( cloudberry=(15+silverfin) marigold<1 / 4 \). However, for any \( cloudberry \leqq 1 / 4 \), we have \( u_{5} \geqq 7 / 64 \) and \( gildedark\left(sandstone \wedge A_{2} \wedge A_{3} \wedge A_{4} \wedge A_{5}\right)=7 marigold<7 / 64 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-3. Suppose that we have \( nothingnum \) events \( nonhappena, \ldots, nonhappenb \), each of which has probability at least \( 1-largeramount \) of occurring, where \( largeramount<1 / 4 \). Further suppose that \( nonhappena \) and \( A \), are mutually independent if \( |outputabc-outputdef|>1 \), although \( nonhappena \) and \( A_{i+1} \) may be dependent. Assume as known that the recurrence \( downgoalx=downgoaly-largeramount downgoalz, downgoalw=1, downgoalv=1-largeramount \), defines positive real numbers \( downgoaly \) for \( outputghi=0,1, \ldots \). Show that the probability of all of \( nonhappena, \ldots, nonhappenb \) occurring is at least \( u_{n} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3.
The statement to be proved is false for \( nothingnum \geqq 5 \) unless the hypothesis is strengthened to state that \( A_{\text {, }} \) is independent of the conjunction of \( nonhappena, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{t-2} \) for \( 3 \leqq outputabc \leqq nothingnum \).

The following counterexample with \( nothingnum=5 \) was furnished by Professor David M. Bloom of Brooklyn College. Let \( smalleramt=33 / 37 \) and \( outputghi=1 /(64+smalleramt) \). Let \( improbability\left(nonhappenc\right) \) be the sum of the numbers in the second row of the following table for which \( nonhappenc \) appears in the heading:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|}
A_{1} A_{2} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{4} & A_{1} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{1} A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{1} A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} & A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} \\
12 outputghi & 3 outputghi & 6 outputghi & 7 outputghi & 12 outputghi & 6 outputghi
\end{array} \right\rvert\,
\]

Then each \( improbability\left(nonhappenc\right) \) is \( 49 outputghi \) and \( improbability\left(nonhappenc \wedge nonhappenc\right)=37 outputghi \) for all \( outputabc, outputdef \) with \( |outputabc-outputdef|>1 \). Since \( (49 outputghi)^{2}=37 outputghi \), the original independence hypothesis holds. Also, \( improbability\left(nonhappenc\right)=1-largeramount \), where \( largeramount=(15+smalleramt) outputghi<1 / 4 \). However, for any \( largeramount \leqq 1 / 4 \), we have \( u_{5} \geqq 7 / 64 \) and \( improbability\left(nonhappena \wedge A_{2} \wedge A_{3} \wedge A_{4} \wedge A_{5}\right)=7 outputghi<7 / 64 \).

garbled_string.question: B-3. Suppose that we have \( qzxwvtnp \) events \( hjgrksla, \ldots, qnvertds \), each of which has probability at least \( 1-bczvmpqs \) of occurring, where \( bczvmpqs<1 / 4 \). Further suppose that \( hjgrksla \) and \( A \), are mutually independent if \( |vnaktsre-plomxcza|>1 \), although \( hjgrksla \) and \( A_{vnaktsre+1} \) may be dependent. Assume as known that the recurrence \( cbyfutop=owileprt-bczvmpqs fjxqneus, poylsmna=1, xgralbei=1-bczvmpqs \), defines positive real numbers \( owileprt \) for \( rtghyuio=0,1, \ldots \). Show that the probability of all of \( hjgrksla, \ldots, qnvertds \) occurring is at least \( u_{qzxwvtnp} \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-3.
The statement to be proved is false for \( qzxwvtnp \geqq 5 \) unless the hypothesis is strengthened to state that \( A_{\text {, }} \) is independent of the conjunction of \( hjgrksla, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{t-2} \) for \( 3 \leqq vnaktsre \leqq qzxwvtnp \).

The following counterexample with \( qzxwvtnp=5 \) was furnished by Professor David M. Bloom of Brooklyn College. Let \( ydkerpqn=33 / 37 \) and \( rtghyuio=1 /(64+ydkerpqn) \). Let \( zaqmtciv\left(kdfghplm\right) \) be the sum of the numbers in the second row of the following table for which \( kdfghplm \) appears in the heading:
\[
\left.\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|}
hjgrksla A_{2} A_{4} A_{5} & A_{4} & hjgrksla A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & hjgrksla A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} & hjgrksla A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} & A_{2} A_{3} A_{4} A_{5} \\
12 rtghyuio & 3 rtghyuio & 6 rtghyuio & 7 rtghyuio & 12 rtghyuio & 6 rtghyuio
\end{array} \right\rvert\,
\]

Then each \( zaqmtciv\left(kdfghplm\right) \) is \( 49 rtghyuio \) and \( zaqmtciv\left(kdfghplm \wedge kdfghplm\right)=37 rtghyuio \) for all \( vnaktsre, plomxcza \) with \( |vnaktsre-plomxcza|>1 \). Since \( (49 rtghyuio)^{2}=37 rtghyuio \), the original independence hypothesis holds. Also, \( zaqmtciv\left(kdfghplm\right)=1-bczvmpqs \), where \( bczvmpqs=(15+ydkerpqn) rtghyuio<1 / 4 \). However, for any \( bczvmpqs \leqq 1 / 4 \), we have \( u_{5} \geqq 7 / 64 \) and \( zaqmtciv\left(hjgrksla \wedge A_{2} \wedge A_{3} \wedge A_{4} \wedge A_{5}\right)=7 rtghyuio<7 / 64 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $r$ be an integer with $r\ge 1$ and let $a$ be a real number that satisfies  
\[
0<a<\frac{1}{\,r+1\,}.
\]
On a common probability space consider $n$ events  
\[
A_{1},A_{2},\dots ,A_{n}\qquad(n\ge r+2)
\]
that fulfil the following two requirements.

(Dep)  (strong $r$-dependence)  
For every index $i$ and every subset  
\[
J\subseteq\{1,2,\dots ,n\}\setminus\{i\}\quad\text{with}\quad |i-j|>r\;\;\forall j\in J ,
\]
the event $A_{i}$ is independent of the event $\displaystyle\bigcap_{j\in J}A_{j}$.

(Prob)  Each single event is highly probable:
\[
\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\ge 1-a\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\]

Define the real sequence $(u_{k})_{k\ge 0}$ by  
\[
u_{0}=1,\qquad
u_{k}=1-ka\;(1\le k\le r),\qquad
u_{k}=u_{k-1}-a\,u_{k-r-1}\quad(k\ge r+1).
\tag{1}
\]

Prove that for every $n\ge r+2$
\[
\boxed{\;
\mathbb{P}\bigl(A_{1}\wedge A_{2}\wedge\cdots\wedge A_{n}\bigr)\;\ge\;u_{n}\;}
\tag{2}
\]

(When $u_{n}<0$ the inequality is trivial.  One easily checks that $u_{n}\ge 0$ whenever $a\le \tfrac{1}{4(r+1)}$.)

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
B_{i}:=A_{i}^{c}\quad\text{(``failure'' events),}\qquad
E_{k}:=\bigcap_{i=1}^{k}A_{i}\;(k\ge 0),\;E_{0}:=\Omega .
\]

Step 1.  The dependency graph for the family $\{B_{i}\}$.

Define the graph $G_{n}^{(r)}=(V,E)$ by  
\[
V=\{1,2,\dots ,n\},\qquad\{i,j\}\in E\;\Longleftrightarrow\;0<|i-j|\le r .
\]
That is, vertices whose indices differ by at most $r$ are adjacent.

We claim that $G_{n}^{(r)}$ is a (proper) dependency graph for the family
$\{B_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$, i.e. that

\[
\text{if}\quad J\subseteq V\setminus\{i\}\;\;\text{satisfies}\;\;
|i-j|>r\ \forall j\in J,
\quad\text{then}\quad
B_{i}\ \text{is independent of}\ \bigcap_{j\in J}B_{j}.
\tag{3}
\]

Proof of the claim.  
Fix such $i$ and $J$ and put $C:=\bigcap_{j\in J}B_{j}$.
By definition $C$ and $A_{i}$ concern disjoint ``$r$-neighbour\-hoods'' of the index line.
Using the elementary relation
$B_{i}=A_{i}^{c}$ we write
\[
\mathbb{P}(B_{i}\cap C)=\mathbb{P}(C)-\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap C).
\tag{4}
\]
For every subset $T\subseteq J$ set $A_{T}:=\bigcap_{j\in T}A_{j}$.  
Because $|i-j|>r$ for all $j\in J$, the hypothesis (Dep) implies
$\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{T})=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\,\mathbb{P}(A_{T})$ for
every $T\subseteq J$.
Via inclusion-exclusion we have
\[
\mathbb{P}(C)=\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{T}),\qquad
\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap C)=\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{T})
=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{T})
=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\mathbb{P}(C).
\]
Insert this identity into (4):
\[
\mathbb{P}(B_{i}\cap C)=\bigl(1-\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\bigr)\mathbb{P}(C)=\mathbb{P}(B_{i})\mathbb{P}(C),
\]
which is (3).  Hence $G_{n}^{(r)}$ is indeed a dependency graph for the
$B_{i}$'s.  \hfill $\square$

Because of (Prob) we have
\[
\mathbb{P}(B_{i})=1-\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\le a\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\tag{5}
\]

Step 2.  Shearer's bound.

Let $I\subseteq V$ be an independent set in $G_{n}^{(r)}$.
Denote by $\Phi_{G}(t)=\sum_{I\text{ indep}}t^{|I|}$ the independent-set
polynomial of a finite graph $G$.
The multivariate form of Shearer's theorem (1985) implies that for any
family of events whose dependency graph is $G$ and numbers
$0\le p_{v}\le 1$ with $\mathbb{P}(B_{v})\le p_{v}$ we have
\[
\mathbb{P}\Bigl(\bigcap_{v\in V}\overline{B_{v}}\Bigr)\;\ge\;
\Phi_{G}(-p_{1},\dots ,-p_{|V|}).
\tag{6}
\]

Apply (6) with $G=G_{n}^{(r)}$ and $p_{v}\equiv a$
(using (5) and Step 1):
\[
\mathbb{P}(E_{n})=\mathbb{P}\Bigl(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n}\overline{B_{i}}\Bigr)
\;\ge\;
\Phi_{G_{n}^{(r)}}(-a).
\tag{7}
\]

Step 3.  Evaluation of $\Phi_{G_{k}^{(r)}}(-a)$.

Call a subset $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,k\}$ $r$-sparse if
any two of its elements differ by more than $r$ (equivalently,
$I$ is independent in $G_{k}^{(r)}$).
Define
\[
w_{k}:=\sum_{I\text{ $r$-sparse in }\{1,\dots ,k\}}(-a)^{|I|}
      =\Phi_{G_{k}^{(r)}}(-a)\qquad(k\ge 0).
\tag{8}
\]

Lemma.  $(w_{k})_{k\ge 0}$ satisfies the recurrence (1) and
$w_{k}=u_{k}$ for every $k$.

Proof.  
The initial values $w_{0}=1$ and $w_{k}=1-ka$ for $1\le k\le r$
are immediate.  
For $k\ge r+1$ split each $r$-sparse $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,k\}$
according to whether $k\in I$.  
If $k\notin I$ the contribution is $w_{k-1}$.
If $k\in I$ then none of $k-r,\dots ,k-1$ belongs to $I$ and
$I\setminus\{k\}$ is an $r$-sparse subset of $\{1,\dots ,k-r-1\}$,
contributing $(-a)w_{k-r-1}$.
Thus
\[
w_{k}=w_{k-1}-a\,w_{k-r-1},\qquad k\ge r+1,
\]
which is precisely (1).  By induction $w_{k}=u_{k}$ for all $k$. \hfill $\square$

Step 4.  Completion.

Combine (7), (8) and the lemma:
\[
\mathbb{P}(E_{n})\;\ge\;w_{n}=u_{n}.
\]
This is exactly inequality (2), completing the proof. \hfill $\blacksquare$

%--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order dependence.  
   • The original problem dealt with 1-dependence; here we allow any fixed
     r ≥ 1.  The dependence graph therefore has bandwidth r instead of 1,
     forcing us to track the interaction of up to r+1 consecutive events.

2. A longer, variable-order recurrence.  
   • The lower-bound sequence (u_k) is no longer second-order
     (Fibonacci-type) but has order r+1.  Handling its positivity and relating
     it to probabilities requires keeping r previous terms in the induction
     instead of just one.

3. Sharper probability bookkeeping.  
   • To control the error term we must isolate a block of exactly r previous
     events and show that everything earlier is independent of A_k.
     This demands a careful decomposition (Eq. (4)) that is absent in the
     original solution.

4. Parameter restrictions and stability.  
   • Proving that the recurrence remains positive for all k forces the extra
     technical condition a < 1/(r+2) and brings in simple but non-trivial
     analytic arguments, while the original statement required only a < 1/4.

5. Conceptual enlargement.  
   • When r = 1 the statement collapses to the classical
     u_{k}=u_{k−1}−a u_{k−2} bound; every larger r introduces a genuinely
     new layer of complexity both in combinatorial dependence and in the
     algebra of the bounding sequence.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially harder: it generalises the
original path-graph problem to arbitrary dependence range, lengthens the
recurrence, and obliges the solver to juggle r+1 interacting probability
classes instead of two.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $r$ be an integer with $r\ge 1$ and let $a$ be a real number that satisfies  
\[
0<a<\frac{1}{\,r+1\,}.
\]
On a common probability space consider $n$ events  
\[

(Dep)  (strong $r$-dependence)

%--------------------------------------------------------------------

Define the graph $G_{n}^{(r)}=(V,E)$ by

\]

We claim that $G_{n}^{(r)}$ is a (proper) dependency graph for the family
$\{B_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$, i.e. that

\[
\text{if}\quad J\subseteq V\setminus\{i\}\;\;\text{satisfies}\;\;
|i-j|>r\ \forall j\in J,
\quad\text{then}\quad
B_{i}\ \text{is independent of}\ \bigcap_{j\in J}B_{j}.
\tag{3}
\]

Proof of the claim.

\]

Because $|i-j|>r$ for all $j\in J$, the hypothesis (Dep) implies
$\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{T})=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\,\mathbb{P}(A_{T})$ for
every $T\subseteq J$.
Via inclusion-exclusion we have
\[
\mathbb{P}(C)=\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{T}),\qquad
\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap C)=\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{T})
=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\sum_{T\subseteq J}(-1)^{|T|}\mathbb{P}(A_{T})
=\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\mathbb{P}(C).
\]
Insert this identity into (4):
\[
\mathbb{P}(B_{i}\cap C)=\bigl(1-\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\bigr)\mathbb{P}(C)=\mathbb{P}(B_{i})\mathbb{P}(C),
\]

\[
\mathbb{P}(B_{i})=1-\mathbb{P}(A_{i})\le a\qquad(1\le i\le n).
\tag{5}
\]

Step 2.  Shearer's bound.

Let $I\subseteq V$ be an independent set in $G_{n}^{(r)}$.
Denote by $\Phi_{G}(t)=\sum_{I\text{ indep}}t^{|I|}$ the independent-set
polynomial of a finite graph $G$.
The multivariate form of Shearer's theorem (1985) implies that for any
family of events whose dependency graph is $G$ and numbers
$0\le p_{v}\le 1$ with $\mathbb{P}(B_{v})\le p_{v}$ we have
\[

\Phi_{G}(-p_{1},\dots ,-p_{|V|}).
\tag{6}
\]

Apply (6) with $G=G_{n}^{(r)}$ and $p_{v}\equiv a$
(using (5) and Step 1):
\[
\mathbb{P}(E_{n})=\mathbb{P}\Bigl(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n}\overline{B_{i}}\Bigr)
\;\ge\;
\Phi_{G_{n}^{(r)}}(-a).
\tag{7}
\]

Step 3.  Evaluation of $\Phi_{G_{k}^{(r)}}(-a)$.

Call a subset $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,k\}$ $r$-sparse if
any two of its elements differ by more than $r$ (equivalently,
$I$ is independent in $G_{k}^{(r)}$).
Define
\[
w_{k}:=\sum_{I\text{ $r$-sparse in }\{1,\dots ,k\}}(-a)^{|I|}
      =\Phi_{G_{k}^{(r)}}(-a)\qquad(k\ge 0).
\tag{8}
\]

Lemma.  $(w_{k})_{k\ge 0}$ satisfies the recurrence (1) and
$w_{k}=u_{k}$ for every $k$.

Proof.  
The initial values $w_{0}=1$ and $w_{k}=1-ka$ for $1\le k\le r$
are immediate.  
For $k\ge r+1$ split each $r$-sparse $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,k\}$
according to whether $k\in I$.  
If $k\notin I$ the contribution is $w_{k-1}$.
If $k\in I$ then none of $k-r,\dots ,k-1$ belongs to $I$ and
$I\setminus\{k\}$ is an $r$-sparse subset of $\{1,\dots ,k-r-1\}$,
contributing $(-a)w_{k-r-1}$.
Thus
\[
w_{k}=w_{k-1}-a\,w_{k-r-1},\qquad k\ge r+1,
\]
which is precisely (1).  By induction $w_{k}=u_{k}$ for all $k$. \hfill $\square$

Step 4.  Completion.

Combine (7), (8) and the lemma:
\[
\mathbb{P}(E_{n})\;\ge\;w_{n}=u_{n}.
\]
This is exactly inequality (2), completing the proof. \hfill $\blacksquare$

%--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order dependence.  
   • The original problem dealt with 1-dependence; here we allow any fixed
     r ≥ 1.  The dependence graph therefore has bandwidth r instead of 1,
     forcing us to track the interaction of up to r+1 consecutive events.

2. A longer, variable-order recurrence.  
   • The lower-bound sequence (u_k) is no longer second-order
     (Fibonacci-type) but has order r+1.  Handling its positivity and relating
     it to probabilities requires keeping r previous terms in the induction
     instead of just one.

3. Sharper probability bookkeeping.  
   • To control the error term we must isolate a block of exactly r previous
     events and show that everything earlier is independent of A_k.
     This demands a careful decomposition (Eq. (4)) that is absent in the
     original solution.

4. Parameter restrictions and stability.  
   • Proving that the recurrence remains positive for all k forces the extra
     technical condition a < 1/(r+2) and brings in simple but non-trivial
     analytic arguments, while the original statement required only a < 1/4.

5. Conceptual enlargement.  
   • When r = 1 the statement collapses to the classical
     u_{k}=u_{k−1}−a u_{k−2} bound; every larger r introduces a genuinely
     new layer of complexity both in combinatorial dependence and in the
     algebra of the bounding sequence.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially harder: it generalises the
original path-graph problem to arbitrary dependence range, lengthens the
recurrence, and obliges the solver to juggle r+1 interacting probability
classes instead of two.
